class BlueprintSetupState:
    """Temporary holder object for registering a blueprint with the
    application.  An instance of this class is created by the
    :meth:`~flask.Blueprint.make_setup_state` method and later passed
    to all register callback functions.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        blueprint: "Blueprint",
        app: "Flask",
        options: t.Any,
        first_registration: bool,
    ) -> None:
        #: a reference to the current application
        self.app = app

        #: a reference to the blueprint that created this setup state.
        self.blueprint = blueprint

        #: a dictionary with all options that were passed to the
        #: :meth:`~flask.Flask.register_blueprint` method.
        self.options = options

        #: as blueprints can be registered multiple times with the
        #: application and not everything wants to be registered
        #: multiple times on it, this attribute can be used to figure
        #: out if the blueprint was registered in the past already.
        self.first_registration = first_registration

        subdomain = self.options.get("subdomain")
        if subdomain is None:
            subdomain = self.blueprint.subdomain

        #: The subdomain that the blueprint should be active for, ``None``
        #: otherwise.
        self.subdomain = subdomain

        url_prefix = self.options.get("url_prefix")
        if url_prefix is None:
            url_prefix = self.blueprint.url_prefix
        #: The prefix that should be used for all URLs defined on the
        #: blueprint.
        self.url_prefix = url_prefix

        self.name_prefix = self.options.get("name_prefix", "")

        #: A dictionary with URL defaults that is added to each and every
        #: URL that was defined with the blueprint.
        self.url_defaults = dict(self.blueprint.url_values_defaults)
        self.url_defaults.update(self.options.get("url_defaults", ()))

    def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
        view_func: t.Optional[t.Callable] = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        """A helper method to register a rule (and optionally a view function)
        to the application.  The endpoint is automatically prefixed with the
        blueprint's name.
        """
        if self.url_prefix is not None:
            if rule:
                rule = "/".join((self.url_prefix.rstrip("/"), rule.lstrip("/")))
            else:
                rule = self.url_prefix
        options.setdefault("subdomain", self.subdomain)
        if endpoint is None:
            endpoint = _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func)  # type: ignore
        defaults = self.url_defaults
        if "defaults" in options:
            defaults = dict(defaults, **options.pop("defaults"))
        self.app.add_url_rule(
            rule,
            f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}",
            view_func,
            defaults=defaults,
            **options,
        )


class Blueprint(Scaffold):
    """Represents a blueprint, a collection of routes and other
    app-related functions that can be registered on a real application
    later.

    A blueprint is an object that allows defining application functions
    without requiring an application object ahead of time. It uses the
    same decorators as :class:`~flask.Flask`, but defers the need for an
    application by recording them for later registration.

    Decorating a function with a blueprint creates a deferred function
    that is called with :class:`~flask.blueprints.BlueprintSetupState`
    when the blueprint is registered on an application.

    See :doc:`/blueprints` for more information.

    :param name: The name of the blueprint. Will be prepended to each
        endpoint name.
    :param import_name: The name of the blueprint package, usually
        ``__name__``. This helps locate the ``root_path`` for the
        blueprint.
    :param static_folder: A folder with static files that should be
        served by the blueprint's static route. The path is relative to
        the blueprint's root path. Blueprint static files are disabled
        by default.
    :param static_url_path: The url to serve static files from.
        Defaults to ``static_folder``. If the blueprint does not have
        a ``url_prefix``, the app's static route will take precedence,
        and the blueprint's static files won't be accessible.
    :param template_folder: A folder with templates that should be added
        to the app's template search path. The path is relative to the
        blueprint's root path. Blueprint templates are disabled by
        default. Blueprint templates have a lower precedence than those
        in the app's templates folder.
    :param url_prefix: A path to prepend to all of the blueprint's URLs,
        to make them distinct from the rest of the app's routes.
    :param subdomain: A subdomain that blueprint routes will match on by
        default.
    :param url_defaults: A dict of default values that blueprint routes
        will receive by default.
    :param root_path: By default, the blueprint will automatically set
        this based on ``import_name``. In certain situations this
        automatic detection can fail, so the path can be specified
        manually instead.

    .. versionchanged:: 1.1.0
        Blueprints have a ``cli`` group to register nested CLI commands.
        The ``cli_group`` parameter controls the name of the group under
        the ``flask`` command.

    .. versionadded:: 0.7
    """

    warn_on_modifications = False
    _got_registered_once = False

    #: Blueprint local JSON encoder class to use. Set to ``None`` to use
    #: the app's :class:`~flask.Flask.json_encoder`.
    json_encoder = None
    #: Blueprint local JSON decoder class to use. Set to ``None`` to use
    #: the app's :class:`~flask.Flask.json_decoder`.
    json_decoder = None

    def __init__(
        self,
        name: str,
        import_name: str,
        static_folder: t.Optional[str] = None,
        static_url_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
        template_folder: t.Optional[str] = None,
        url_prefix: t.Optional[str] = None,
        subdomain: t.Optional[str] = None,
        url_defaults: t.Optional[dict] = None,
        root_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
        cli_group: t.Optional[str] = _sentinel,  # type: ignore
    ):
        super().__init__(
            import_name=import_name,
            static_folder=static_folder,
            static_url_path=static_url_path,
            template_folder=template_folder,
            root_path=root_path,
        )
        self.name = name
        self.url_prefix = url_prefix
        self.subdomain = subdomain
        self.deferred_functions: t.List[DeferredSetupFunction] = []

        if url_defaults is None:
            url_defaults = {}

        self.url_values_defaults = url_defaults
        self.cli_group = cli_group
        self._blueprints: t.List[t.Tuple["Blueprint", dict]] = []

    def _is_setup_finished(self) -> bool:
        return self.warn_on_modifications and self._got_registered_once

    def record(self, func: t.Callable) -> None:
        """Registers a function that is called when the blueprint is
        registered on the application.  This function is called with the
        state as argument as returned by the :meth:`make_setup_state`
        method.
        """
        if self._got_registered_once and self.warn_on_modifications:
            from warnings import warn

            warn(
                Warning(
                    "The blueprint was already registered once but is"
                    " getting modified now. These changes will not show"
                    " up."
                )
            )
        self.deferred_functions.append(func)

    def record_once(self, func: t.Callable) -> None:
        """Works like :meth:`record` but wraps the function in another
        function that will ensure the function is only called once.  If the
        blueprint is registered a second time on the application, the
        function passed is not called.
        """

        def wrapper(state: BlueprintSetupState) -> None:
            if state.first_registration:
                func(state)

        return self.record(update_wrapper(wrapper, func))

    def make_setup_state(
        self, app: "Flask", options: dict, first_registration: bool = False
    ) -> BlueprintSetupState:
        """Creates an instance of :meth:`~flask.blueprints.BlueprintSetupState`
        object that is later passed to the register callback functions.
        Subclasses can override this to return a subclass of the setup state.
        """
        return BlueprintSetupState(self, app, options, first_registration)

    def register_blueprint(self, blueprint: "Blueprint", **options: t.Any) -> None:
        """Register a :class:`~flask.Blueprint` on this blueprint. Keyword
        arguments passed to this method will override the defaults set
        on the blueprint.

        .. versionadded:: 2.0
        """
        self._blueprints.append((blueprint, options))

    def register(self, app: "Flask", options: dict) -> None:
        """Called by :meth:`Flask.register_blueprint` to register all
        views and callbacks registered on the blueprint with the
        application. Creates a :class:`.BlueprintSetupState` and calls
        each :meth:`record` callbackwith it.

        :param app: The application this blueprint is being registered
            with.
        :param options: Keyword arguments forwarded from
            :meth:`~Flask.register_blueprint`.
        :param first_registration: Whether this is the first time this
            blueprint has been registered on the application.
        """
        first_registration = False

        if self.name in app.blueprints:
            assert app.blueprints[self.name] is self, (
                "A name collision occurred between blueprints"
                f" {self!r} and {app.blueprints[self.name]!r}."
                f" Both share the same name {self.name!r}."
                f" Blueprints that are created on the fly need unique"
                f" names."
            )
        else:
            app.blueprints[self.name] = self
            first_registration = True

        self._got_registered_once = True
        state = self.make_setup_state(app, options, first_registration)

        if self.has_static_folder:
            state.add_url_rule(
                f"{self.static_url_path}/<path:filename>",
                view_func=self.send_static_file,
                endpoint="static",
            )

        # Merge blueprint data into parent.
        if first_registration:

            def extend(bp_dict, parent_dict):
                for key, values in bp_dict.items():
                    key = self.name if key is None else f"{self.name}.{key}"

                    parent_dict[key].extend(values)

            for key, value in self.error_handler_spec.items():
                key = self.name if key is None else f"{self.name}.{key}"
                value = defaultdict(
                    dict,
                    {
                        code: {
                            exc_class: func for exc_class, func in code_values.items()
                        }
                        for code, code_values in value.items()
                    },
                )
                app.error_handler_spec[key] = value

            for endpoint, func in self.view_functions.items():
                app.view_functions[endpoint] = func

            extend(self.before_request_funcs, app.before_request_funcs)
            extend(self.after_request_funcs, app.after_request_funcs)
            extend(
                self.teardown_request_funcs,
                app.teardown_request_funcs,
            )
            extend(self.url_default_functions, app.url_default_functions)
            extend(self.url_value_preprocessors, app.url_value_preprocessors)
            extend(self.template_context_processors, app.template_context_processors)

        for deferred in self.deferred_functions:
            deferred(state)

        cli_resolved_group = options.get("cli_group", self.cli_group)

        if self.cli.commands:
            if cli_resolved_group is None:
                app.cli.commands.update(self.cli.commands)
            elif cli_resolved_group is _sentinel:
                self.cli.name = self.name
                app.cli.add_command(self.cli)
            else:
                self.cli.name = cli_resolved_group
                app.cli.add_command(self.cli)

        for blueprint, bp_options in self._blueprints:
            url_prefix = options.get("url_prefix", "")
            if "url_prefix" in bp_options:
                url_prefix = (
                    url_prefix.rstrip("/") + "/" + bp_options["url_prefix"].lstrip("/")
                )

            bp_options["url_prefix"] = url_prefix
            bp_options["name_prefix"] = options.get("name_prefix", "") + self.name + "."
            blueprint.register(app, bp_options)

    def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
        view_func: t.Optional[t.Callable] = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        """Like :meth:`Flask.add_url_rule` but for a blueprint.  The endpoint for
        the :func:`url_for` function is prefixed with the name of the blueprint.
        """
        if endpoint:
            assert "." not in endpoint, "Blueprint endpoints should not contain dots"
        if view_func and hasattr(view_func, "__name__"):
            assert (
                "." not in view_func.__name__
            ), "Blueprint view function name should not contain dots"
        self.record(lambda s: s.add_url_rule(rule, endpoint, view_func, **options))

    def app_template_filter(self, name: t.Optional[str] = None) -> t.Callable:
        """Register a custom template filter, available application wide.  Like
        :meth:`Flask.template_filter` but for a blueprint.

        :param name: the optional name of the filter, otherwise the
                     function name will be used.
        """

        def decorator(f: TemplateFilterCallable) -> TemplateFilterCallable:
            self.add_app_template_filter(f, name=name)
            return f

        return decorator

    def add_app_template_filter(
        self, f: TemplateFilterCallable, name: t.Optional[str] = None
    ) -> None:
        """Register a custom template filter, available application wide.  Like
        :meth:`Flask.add_template_filter` but for a blueprint.  Works exactly
        like the :meth:`app_template_filter` decorator.

        :param name: the optional name of the filter, otherwise the
                     function name will be used.
        """

        def register_template(state: BlueprintSetupState) -> None:
            state.app.jinja_env.filters[name or f.__name__] = f

        self.record_once(register_template)

    def iter_blueprints(self) -> t.ValuesView["Blueprint"]:
        """Iterates over all blueprints by the order they were registered.

    @setupmethod
    def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
        view_func: t.Optional[t.Callable] = None,
        provide_automatic_options: t.Optional[bool] = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        if endpoint is None:
            endpoint = _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func)  # type: ignore
        options["endpoint"] = endpoint
        methods = options.pop("methods", None)

        # if the methods are not given and the view_func object knows its
        # methods we can use that instead.  If neither exists, we go with
        # a tuple of only ``GET`` as default.
        if methods is None:
            methods = getattr(view_func, "methods", None) or ("GET",)
        if isinstance(methods, str):
            raise TypeError(
                "Allowed methods must be a list of strings, for"
                ' example: @app.route(..., methods=["POST"])'
            )
        methods = {item.upper() for item in methods}

        # Methods that should always be added
        required_methods = set(getattr(view_func, "required_methods", ()))

        # starting with Flask 0.8 the view_func object can disable and
        # force-enable the automatic options handling.
        if provide_automatic_options is None:
            provide_automatic_options = getattr(
                view_func, "provide_automatic_options", None
            )

        if provide_automatic_options is None:
            if "OPTIONS" not in methods:
                provide_automatic_options = True
                required_methods.add("OPTIONS")
            else:
                provide_automatic_options = False

        # Add the required methods now.
        methods |= required_methods

        rule = self.url_rule_class(rule, methods=methods, **options)
        rule.provide_automatic_options = provide_automatic_options  # type: ignore

        self.url_map.add(rule)
        if view_func is not None:
            old_func = self.view_functions.get(endpoint)
            if old_func is not None and old_func != view_func:
                raise AssertionError(
                    "View function mapping is overwriting an existing"
                    f" endpoint function: {endpoint}"
                )
            self.view_functions[endpoint] = view_func

def _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func: t.Callable) -> str:
    """Internal helper that returns the default endpoint for a given
    function.  This always is the function name.
    """
    assert view_func is not None, "expected view func if endpoint is not provided."
    return view_func.__name__


def _matching_loader_thinks_module_is_package(loader, mod_name):
    """Attempt to figure out if the given name is a package or a module.

    :param: loader: The loader that handled the name.
    :param mod_name: The name of the package or module.
    """
    # Use loader.is_package if it's available.
    if hasattr(loader, "is_package"):
        return loader.is_package(mod_name)

    cls = type(loader)

    # NamespaceLoader doesn't implement is_package, but all names it
    # loads must be packages.
    if cls.__module__ == "_frozen_importlib" and cls.__name__ == "NamespaceLoader":
        return True

    # Otherwise we need to fail with an error that explains what went
    # wrong.
    raise AttributeError(
        f"'{cls.__name__}.is_package()' must be implemented for PEP 302"
        f" import hooks."
    )


def _find_package_path(root_mod_name):
    """Find the path that contains the package or module."""
    try:
        spec = importlib.util.find_spec(root_mod_name)

        if spec is None:
            raise ValueError("not found")
    # ImportError: the machinery told us it does not exist
    # ValueError:
    #    - the module name was invalid

- Parent methods to inspect (from Scaffold/Flask): Flask.add_url_rule, Flask.view_functions handling, app.inject_url_defaults, url_for resolution, app.error_handler_spec keys, and other merges in Blueprint.register.

  - BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule builds endpoint strings: f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}" — with the fix, blueprint.name contains no dots so final endpoint string is unambiguous.
  - In register(), merge code forms keys with f"{self.name}.{key}" — again safe because name has no dots.
  - Flask.add_url_rule (in app.py) receives endpoint names as-is and stores view_functions[endpoint] — it already allows dots in endpoint strings, which is necessary because final endpoint strings used by nested blueprints contain dots (e.g., "parent.child.view"). Flask.add_url_rule does not assume anything about presence/absence of dots in the endpoint and will work properly.

- Concrete substitution trace example for the most important complementary operation (BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule -> Flask.add_url_rule):
  - Variables:
    - self.name_prefix: some prefix possibly like "parent." or "", constructed at registration time
    - self.blueprint.name: (with the fix) a string that contains no dots (e.g. "child")
    - endpoint: endpoint name provided by blueprint's add_url_rule (must not contain dots; this was already asserted in Blueprint.add_url_rule)
  - Computation:
    - final endpoint = f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}" -> e.g. "parent.child.view"
  - Parent (Flask) receives this endpoint and stores view_functions["parent.child.view"] = view_func.
  - Expected result: endpoints formed by concatenating blueprint names and endpoint names with '.' separators. With both sides validating (Blueprint forbids '.' in endpoint and we now forbid '.' in blueprint name) the result is unambiguous and correct.

- Conclusion: The complementary operations are consistent with the new validation — no additional overrides are required in the parent class.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Review of Blueprint methods:
  - __init__: we add the assertion — it now refuses dotted names.
  - register: uses self.name to form keys and name_prefix; still works because name now guaranteed to have no dots.
  - add_url_rule (Blueprint): already asserts '.' not in endpoint and view function name; remains correct.
  - BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule: still composes endpoint using '.' separator; still correct because both name and endpoint cannot contain '.'.
  - The app_* decorator methods record/record_once logic remains unaffected.
- No method in Blueprint code needs to change to handle the new restriction; they will all continue to function.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- I inspected parent Scaffold methods that might compute names or parse endpoints. None need to be overridden by Blueprint to account for the new validation.
- The only additional required changes are at the callers/consumers of Blueprint construction: tests and code that create Blueprint instances with dotted names must be updated to either:
  - use nested blueprints via Blueprint.register_blueprint (the intended pattern now), or
  - rename the blueprint to avoid '.'.
- Therefore no new methods required in the runtime library. The change is a validation on construction of Blueprint instances.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and changes required)

Root cause
- The codebase uses '.' as a reserved separator when composing nested blueprint keys and endpoint names (e.g., "parent.child.endpoint"). There was an assertion preventing '.' in endpoint names in Blueprint.add_url_rule, but no corresponding assertion preventing '.' in blueprint names themselves. This allowed blueprint names with dots, which conflicts with semantics of nested blueprints and can produce ambiguous or incorrect keys/endpoint names.

Primary fix
- Add validation to Blueprint.__init__ to reject names containing '.':
  - Add assertion (to follow existing style in the module):

        assert "." not in name, "Blueprint names should not contain dots"

  - Place this in src/flask/blueprints.py inside Blueprint.__init__, before setting self.name.

Why this is sufficient
- The other code that composes dotted names assumes blueprint names do not contain dots; by enforcing that invariant at construction, all dotted concatenations remain unambiguous.
- Blueprint.add_url_rule already forbids '.' in endpoint names; together these two checks ensure endpoints like "a.b.c.view" are only produced by nesting Blueprints (via register_blueprint) and not by allowing '.' inside either the blueprint name or endpoint name.

Other affected places that must be changed (not all code, but must be updated in the codebase/test-suite/documentation)
- Tests that create Blueprint instances with dotted names must be updated. Examples from this repo:
  - tests/test_basic.py:
    - around line where bp = flask.Blueprint("foo.bar.baz", __name__, ...)
      - The test expects url_for("foo.bar.baz.view") to work when registering a single Blueprint named "foo.bar.baz". This test will need to be changed to create nested blueprints instead:
        - Create three Blueprints: "foo", "bar", "baz", nest them with register_blueprint and then register the parent blueprint on the app, or otherwise rename the blueprint to not contain dots and construct the nested structure explicitly.
  - tests/test_blueprints.py:
    - lines where "myapp.frontend", "myapp.backend" are used — change to nested blueprints or rename.
  - Any other test file that constructs Blueprint("some.name.with.dots", ...) must be updated similarly.
- Documentation and examples that rely on dotted blueprint names should also be updated to recommend nested blueprints (using Blueprint.register_blueprint) rather than embedding dots in the blueprint name.

Why tests must change
- The new assertion intentionally disallows dotted blueprint names. Existing tests that created dotted names were written before nested blueprint semantics were introduced; they must be transitioned to use the explicit nested-blueprint API to continue to test the same scenarios.

Suggested test updates — concrete guidance
- Replace places like:

    bp = Blueprint("foo.bar.baz", __name__)
    @bp.route("/<page>")
    def view(page): ...
    app.register_blueprint(bp)
    url_for("foo.bar.baz.view")

  with nested blueprint creation:

    grandchild = Blueprint("baz", __name__)
    @grandchild.route("/<page>")
    def view(page): ...
    child = Blueprint("bar", __name__)
    child.register_blueprint(grandchild)
    parent = Blueprint("foo", __name__)
    parent.register_blueprint(child)
    app.register_blueprint(parent)
    url_for("foo.bar.baz.view")

- Or rename blueprint names without dots and adjust expected endpoint names accordingly.

All places to change in code (summary)
- Primary code change:
  - src/flask/blueprints.py: add the assertion in Blueprint.__init__:
    - Add line: assert "." not in name, "Blueprint names should not contain dots"

- Tests & docs:
  - Update tests that create dotted blueprint names to use nested blueprint registration or rename to non-dotted names.
  - Update examples/documentation that show dotted blueprint names to illustrate nested blueprints instead.

Why no other code changes (e.g., Flask or Scaffold) are needed
- Flask (the other Scaffold subclass) already accepts dotted endpoint names because final endpoints for nested blueprints intentionally include dots; this behavior is correct and complementary to the blueprint name validation introduced here. The parent class methods that build and consume endpoints will behave correctly once blueprint names and endpoint names are guaranteed not to contain '.'.

Edge cases, style, and choice of error type
- The repository uses assert for similar checks (e.g., Blueprint.add_url_rule uses assert "." not in endpoint, "Blueprint endpoints should not contain dots"), so I followed the same style and used assert to signal misuse by library clients.
- If you prefer raising ValueError or AssertionError explicitly (so it is not optimized away when Python is run with -O), convert the assertion into raising an explicit AssertionError or ValueError:

    if "." in name:
        raise ValueError("Blueprint names should not contain dots")

This is a stylistic choice; current codebase uses assertions for similar name checks so adding an assert is consistent.

Summary
- Implement the single-line validation in Blueprint.__init__:
  - assert "." not in name, "Blueprint names should not contain dots"
- Update any tests and documentation that construct blueprints with dotted names to use nested-blueprint registration (Blueprint.register_blueprint) or rename blueprints to avoid dots.

If you'd like, I can:
- Draft the exact patch (unified diff) to apply to src/flask/blueprints.py,
- Update a representative test (e.g., the test in tests/test_basic.py that uses "foo.bar.baz") to the nested-blueprint style so the test-suite reflects the new behavior,
- Or instead convert the assert to an explicit ValueError raise if you prefer that style for public API validation. Which would you like me to produce next?

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify each claim in the draft by inspecting the source code for the methods and files referenced, confirm whether the code supports the draft's RootCause, and check for any missing or unsupported claims.

1) Verify the methods and claims by reading source

- Why I fetched src/flask/blueprints.py:
  - To confirm where blueprint.name is assigned and to inspect Blueprint.__init__, Blueprint.add_url_rule, Blueprint.register, and BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule. I expected to confirm whether blueprint names are validated and how final endpoints / name_prefix values are composed.

  Findings (quotes from file src/flask/blueprints.py):
  - Blueprint.__init__ assigns the name without validation:
    - Line 191: self.name = name
    - (context: __init__ spans lines ~171-201; the assignment is at line 191)
  - BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule composes the final endpoint with a '.' and the blueprint.name:
    - Line 101: f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}"
    - (the app.add_url_rule call uses that string as the endpoint argument)
  - Blueprint.register composes name_prefix and also uses f"{self.name}.{key}":
    - Line 350: bp_options["name_prefix"] = options.get("name_prefix", "") + self.name + "."
    - Line 297 (inside extend): key = self.name if key is None else f"{self.name}.{key}"
  - Blueprint.add_url_rule asserts endpoint and view function names contain no '.':
    - Line 364: assert "." not in endpoint, "Blueprint endpoints should not contain dots"
    - Line 367-368: assert "." not in view_func.__name__, "Blueprint view function name should not contain dots"

  Analysis:
  - The code confirms that blueprint.name is assigned directly with no check for '.' (line 191).
  - The code explicitly uses '.' as a separator when creating name_prefix and when composing final endpoint strings (lines 101, 350, 297).
  - The code contains assertions forbidding '.' in endpoint names and in view function __name__ when adding a rule via Blueprint.add_url_rule (lines 364, 367-368).
  - There is no complementary assertion or validation forbidding '.' in the blueprint.name itself in this file.

- Why I fetched src/flask/scaffold.py:
  - To confirm how default endpoints are created (_endpoint_from_view_func) and to check what the Scaffold/Flask base classes do with endpoint strings (e.g., whether '.' is forbidden at app.add_url_rule).
  - I expected to see whether Flask-level code rejects '.' in endpoint names or accepts dotted endpoint names produced by blueprints.

  Findings (quotes from src/flask/scaffold.py):
  - _endpoint_from_view_func returns the function name:
    - Line 738-743: return view_func.__name__
  - Scaffold defines view_functions storage (no special restrictions on '.' in keys):
    - Line 128: self.view_functions: t.Dict[str, t.Callable] = {}

  - Flask.add_url_rule (in app.py) stores endpoint keys in view_functions (see below); Scaffold itself does not implement add_url_rule for the app-level, but provides helpers.

  Analysis:
  - The default endpoint is the function name, and nothing in _endpoint_from_view_func forbids dots in returned endpoints (it simply returns view_func.__name__). However, Blueprint.add_url_rule checks view_func.__name__ for dots before recording.
  - Scaffold does not itself enforce a '.' prohibition on endpoint names.

- Why I fetched src/flask/app.py:
  - To inspect Flask.add_url_rule and register_blueprint to see how the composed endpoint values from BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule get handled at the app level.

  Findings (quotes from src/flask/app.py):
  - Flask.register_blueprint calls blueprint.register(self, options):
    - Line 1023: blueprint.register(self, options)
  - Flask.add_url_rule adds the Rule to the url_map and then sets view_functions[endpoint] = view_func with no check forbidding '.':
    - Line 1081: self.url_map.add(rule)
    - Line 1091-1090: when view_func is provided, it checks for endpoint name collisions and then sets:
      - Line 1089: self.view_functions[endpoint] = view_func

  Analysis:
  - Flask accepts endpoint strings produced by Blueprints (view_functions keys are set directly) and does not itself forbid '.' in endpoint names. That means dotted final endpoints produced by blueprint registration will be accepted by Flask.

- Why I searched tests:
  - To confirm whether test code instantiates Blueprints with dotted names and therefore encodes the opposite assumption (that blueprint names may contain '.').
  - I expected to find tests that construct Blueprints with dotted names.

  Findings (quotes from tests):
  - tests/test_basic.py:
    - Line 1634: bp = flask.Blueprint("foo.bar.baz", __name__, template_folder="template")
    - Later uses app.inject_url_defaults("foo.bar.baz.view", values) (line 1647) which demonstrates tests expecting dotted blueprint names to appear in endpoints.
  - tests/test_blueprints.py:
    - Lines 256-259: frontend = flask.Blueprint("myapp.frontend", __name__); backend = flask.Blueprint("myapp.backend", __name__)
    - Tests then refer to endpoints like "myapp.backend.backend_index" (line 262) and "myapp.frontend.frontend_index" (line 270).

  Analysis:
  - The tests do create Blueprints with dotted names and also expect the dotted-blueprint names to appear in endpoint strings.

2) Verify the RootCause statements against code

The draft RootCause contained multiple numbered findings. I validated each with code:

- Draft RootCause item 1: Blueprint.__init__ assigns self.name without validating for '.'.
  - Verified: line 191 self.name = name. No checks present in __init__. This supports the claim.

- Draft RootCause item 2: Blueprint.add_url_rule asserts endpoint and view function names contain no '.'; symmetric validation for blueprint names is missing.
  - Verified: lines 364 and 367-368 contain the assertions forbidding '.' in endpoint and view func names. There is indeed no complementary check for blueprint.name elsewhere in this file.

- Draft RootCause item 3: BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule composes final endpoint with f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}", assuming blueprint.name has no '.'.
  - Verified: line 101 uses f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}" as the endpoint passed to app.add_url_rule.

- Draft RootCause item 4: Blueprint.register builds bp_options["name_prefix"] with options.get("name_prefix", "") + self.name + "." and composes names with f"{self.name}.{key}"; these assume self.name contains no '.'.
  - Verified: line 350 sets name_prefix with self.name + ".", and line 297 uses f"{self.name}.{key}" for merging mappings. Both use '.' as a separator and therefore assume the separator will not be present in self.name.

- Draft RootCause item 5: Tests instantiate Blueprints with dotted names and will fail if constructor-time validation is added.
  - Verified: tests/test_basic.py and tests/test_blueprints.py show dotted blueprint names (lines cited above). Thus, adding constructor-time validation would change test expectations (draft correctly identifies test coverage impact).

- Draft RootCause item 6: Investigation coverage gap — missing concrete call-graph/execution trace.
  - Verified: the earlier agent reported a get_subgraph failure and no runtime trace was produced. The source inspection shows the string compositions and assertions, but a concrete runtime trace was not recorded. This is an investigation gap, not a code assertion.

3) Check for any unsupported claims in the draft

- The draft claims that Blueprint.add_url_rule's endpoint and view function assertions "encode the complementary assumption that blueprint.name will not contain '.'". This is an inference — code does assert on endpoints and view function names, but it does not explicitly assert that blueprint.name lacks '.'; however, the concatenation sites (f-strings that inject '.' between names) do rely on the delimiter semantics. So the inference is reasonable and supported by the concatenation usage, but strictly speaking the code does not contain an explicit assertion enforcing that assumption.

- The draft also claims that "Other code concatenates blueprint.name with '.' to form fully-qualified endpoint/name_prefix values, so allowing '.' in blueprint names creates ambiguous endpoint identifiers", which the source supports: the exact concatenation expressions are present (lines 101, 297, 350). The claim that this creates ambiguous identifiers is a logical consequence: if '.' is the delimiter, allowing '.' in the components makes the delimiter ambiguous. The code evidence (concatenations and no validation) supports the claim.

No other unsupported claims were found.

4) Important findings from investigation analysis that the draft missed

- The draft already lists the key locations and the tests. The investigation analysis also pointed out that Flask.add_url_rule and Scaffold accept dotted endpoint names (i.e., app-level code does not reject '.'), which the draft implicitly relies on but does not explicitly state. I verified and will include this as evidence: Flask accepts dotted endpoints (no '.'' prohibition at app.add_url_rule).

- The investigation pointed out the register merge logic also uses f"{self.name}.{key}" for other blueprint data (error handlers, before/after request funcs, url default preprocessors). The draft included these but I'll ensure the RootCause lists the extend/merge lines as explicit affected places (line 297 and lines 301-312, 314-325 etc. show various merges). I will ensure all such locations are captured in RootCause with equal weight.

5) Completeness check for secondary findings (same root cause pattern)

- The same root cause pattern (missing validation of '.' in Blueprint.name) appears in multiple code sites that assume the blueprint name is dot-free:
  - Blueprint.__init__ (assignment, missing check) — (line 191)
  - Blueprint.add_url_rule (asserts on endpoint and view func names but not on blueprint.name) — (lines 363-369)
  - BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule (composes final endpoint with blueprint.name + ".") — (line 101)
  - Blueprint.register (sets bp_options["name_prefix"] = ... + self.name + "." and uses f"{self.name}.{key}" while merging many per-blueprint data structures) — (lines 297, 350, and the merging loops at 301-326)
  - Tests in tests/ that create dotted blueprint names — (tests/test_basic.py:1634, tests/test_blueprints.py:256-258 etc.)
  - Flask.app.add_url_rule accepts dotted endpoints and stores them in view_functions; it does not enforce a prohibition on '.' — (app.py lines 1081, 1089)

All of these share the same root cause: the codebase uses '.' as a reserved separator when composing names, but blueprint name validation is missing and tests (and some usage) assume blueprint names may already contain '.'.

6) Impact completeness check: other paths and references

- All code paths that build fully-qualified identifiers involving blueprint.name are in Blueprint.register (merging handlers and functions into the app with keys derived from self.name and self.name + "." + key) and BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule (which creates endpoints passed to app.add_url_rule).
- The app-level code (Flask.add_url_rule) accepts endpoint strings as-is; therefore dotted endpoints produced by blueprints with '.' in their name will be stored and used by url_map and view_functions.
- There is not an existing centralized validation point for blueprint.name; the only relevant checks I see are those in Blueprint.add_url_rule for endpoint and view func names (not blueprint.name). No other class or attribute enforces that blueprint.name is dot-free.

7) Field discipline check (confirming which fields must be edited)

- Description: the draft Description is concise and accurate (it states the problem: blueprint names with '.' are allowed even though '.' is reserved). I will keep it but may tighten wording for clarity.

bug_report.Title: Blueprint names containing '.' are allowed, producing ambiguous nested endpoint names
bug_report.Description: Blueprint.__init__ accepts names that contain '.' even though '.' is used as the reserved separator when composing nested blueprint/name_prefix and endpoint identifiers. Other code constructs fully-qualified identifiers by inserting '.' between blueprint name components, so allowing '.' in blueprint names undermines the delimiter invariant and produces ambiguous endpoint identifiers.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/flask/blueprints.py:Blueprint.__init__ — the constructor assigns self.name = name (line 191) with no validation that the provided name contains no '.'. This encodes the assumption "blueprint.name may include any characters" instead of the reserved-separator invariant.

2) src/flask/blueprints.py:Blueprint.add_url_rule — this method asserts that endpoint and view function __name__ do not contain '.' (lines 364 and 367-368: "assert '.' not in endpoint", "assert '.' not in view_func.__name__"). Those checks encode the complementary assumption that components used to build fully-qualified endpoint names will not contain '.'; however, blueprint.name is not validated to satisfy that same assumption.

3) src/flask/blueprints.py:BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule — the final endpoint argument passed to the application is built with the literal-format expression f"{self.name_prefix}{self.blueprint.name}.{endpoint}" (line 101). This composition uses '.' as the separator between blueprint.name and the local endpoint, and therefore assumes blueprint.name is dot-free.

4) src/flask/blueprints.py:Blueprint.register — several places merge blueprint-scoped data into the application using expressions that hardcode '.' as the separator and rely on a dot-free blueprint.name. Examples include:
   - bp_options["name_prefix"] = options.get("name_prefix", "") + self.name + "." (line 350)
   - key = self.name if key is None else f"{self.name}.{key}" used when merging error handlers and callback mappings (line 297 and surrounding merge loops at lines 301-326).
   These constructs assume self.name contains no '.' so that the inserted '.' is the unique delimiter between namespace components.

5) tests/test_basic.py and tests/test_blueprints.py — test code instantiates Blueprints with dotted names (e.g. "foo.bar.baz" in tests/test_basic.py line 1634 and "myapp.frontend" / "myapp.backend" in tests/test_blueprints.py lines 256-259). These tests assume dotted blueprint names are permitted and that dotted names appear in final endpoints (they reference endpoints like "foo.bar.baz.view" or "myapp.backend.backend_index").

6) Application-level behavior (src/flask/app.py:Flask.add_url_rule) — Flask.add_url_rule adds the rule to the url_map and assigns view_functions[endpoint] = view_func without rejecting '.' in endpoint strings (lines 1081 and 1089), so dotted final endpoints produced by the blueprint code are accepted by Flask. The overall inconsistency is: code composes dotted endpoints and forbids '.' in endpoint/view function names, but does not forbid '.' in blueprint names, producing an ambiguous delimiter invariant.

7) Investigation coverage gap — there is no concrete runtime trace (call-graph/execution trace) recorded showing specific runtime values flowing through the register/add_url_rule path; the source-level inspection shows the compositions and assertions, but a recorded runtime trace is not present in the investigation artifacts.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Blueprint with a dotted name, e.g. flask.Blueprint('foo.bar', __name__).
Define a view and call blueprint.add_url_rule or use the blueprint.route decorator, then register the blueprint on the app with app.register_blueprint(blueprint).
Inspect the application's endpoint keys (e.g. app.view_functions keys or app.url_map) and observe the constructed endpoint/name_prefix that includes the blueprint's '.' characters.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Blueprint creation should present user-visible validation: creating a Blueprint with a name that contains '.' should not be permitted as a valid blueprint name. The user-visible behavior should be that dotted blueprint names are rejected at creation time (constructor raises an immediate error), so composed nested endpoint/name_prefix identifiers remain unambiguous.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Currently, Blueprint.__init__ allows names with '.' and no constructor-time error is raised. Later, registration and url-rule composition use expressions like f"{name_prefix}{blueprint.name}.{endpoint}" and bp_options["name_prefix"] = ... + self.name + ".", which embed the blueprint's '.' characters into final identifiers. Blueprint.add_url_rule asserts that endpoint and view function names contain no '.', creating an inconsistent invariant between allowed blueprint names and forbidden endpoint/view function name characters; tests in the codebase also create dotted blueprint names and expect dotted identifiers to appear.
