class BlueprintSetupState:
    """Temporary holder object for registering a blueprint with the
    application.  An instance of this class is created by the
    :meth:`~flask.Blueprint.make_setup_state` method and later passed
    to all register callback functions.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        blueprint: "Blueprint",
        app: "Flask",
        options: t.Any,
        first_registration: bool,
    ) -> None:
        #: a reference to the current application
        self.app = app

        #: a reference to the blueprint that created this setup state.
        self.blueprint = blueprint

        #: a dictionary with all options that were passed to the
        #: :meth:`~flask.Flask.register_blueprint` method.
        self.options = options

        #: as blueprints can be registered multiple times with the
        #: application and not everything wants to be registered
        #: multiple times on it, this attribute can be used to figure
        #: out if the blueprint was registered in the past already.
        self.first_registration = first_registration

        subdomain = self.options.get("subdomain")
        if subdomain is None:
            subdomain = self.blueprint.subdomain

        #: The subdomain that the blueprint should be active for, ``None``
        #: otherwise.
        self.subdomain = subdomain

        url_prefix = self.options.get("url_prefix")
        if url_prefix is None:
            url_prefix = self.blueprint.url_prefix
        #: The prefix that should be used for all URLs defined on the
        #: blueprint.
        self.url_prefix = url_prefix

        self.name = self.options.get("name", blueprint.name)
        self.name_prefix = self.options.get("name_prefix", "")

        #: A dictionary with URL defaults that is added to each and every
        #: URL that was defined with the blueprint.
        self.url_defaults = dict(self.blueprint.url_values_defaults)
        self.url_defaults.update(self.options.get("url_defaults", ()))

    def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
        view_func: t.Optional[t.Callable] = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        """A helper method to register a rule (and optionally a view function)
        to the application.  The endpoint is automatically prefixed with the
        blueprint's name.
        """
        if self.url_prefix is not None:
            if rule:
                rule = "/".join((self.url_prefix.rstrip("/"), rule.lstrip("/")))
            else:
                rule = self.url_prefix
        options.setdefault("subdomain", self.subdomain)
        if endpoint is None:
            endpoint = _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func)  # type: ignore
        defaults = self.url_defaults
        if "defaults" in options:
            defaults = dict(defaults, **options.pop("defaults"))

        self.app.add_url_rule(
            rule,
            f"{self.name_prefix}.{self.name}.{endpoint}".lstrip("."),
            view_func,
            defaults=defaults,
            **options,
        )


class Blueprint(Scaffold):
    """Represents a blueprint, a collection of routes and other
    app-related functions that can be registered on a real application
    later.

    A blueprint is an object that allows defining application functions
    without requiring an application object ahead of time. It uses the
    same decorators as :class:`~flask.Flask`, but defers the need for an
    application by recording them for later registration.

    Decorating a function with a blueprint creates a deferred function
    that is called with :class:`~flask.blueprints.BlueprintSetupState`
    when the blueprint is registered on an application.

    See :doc:`/blueprints` for more information.

    :param name: The name of the blueprint. Will be prepended to each
        endpoint name.
    :param import_name: The name of the blueprint package, usually
        ``__name__``. This helps locate the ``root_path`` for the
        blueprint.
    :param static_folder: A folder with static files that should be
        served by the blueprint's static route. The path is relative to
        the blueprint's root path. Blueprint static files are disabled
        by default.
    :param static_url_path: The url to serve static files from.
        Defaults to ``static_folder``. If the blueprint does not have
        a ``url_prefix``, the app's static route will take precedence,
        and the blueprint's static files won't be accessible.
    :param template_folder: A folder with templates that should be added
        to the app's template search path. The path is relative to the
        blueprint's root path. Blueprint templates are disabled by
        default. Blueprint templates have a lower precedence than those
        in the app's templates folder.
    :param url_prefix: A path to prepend to all of the blueprint's URLs,
        to make them distinct from the rest of the app's routes.
    :param subdomain: A subdomain that blueprint routes will match on by
        default.
    :param url_defaults: A dict of default values that blueprint routes
        will receive by default.
    :param root_path: By default, the blueprint will automatically set
        this based on ``import_name``. In certain situations this
        automatic detection can fail, so the path can be specified
        manually instead.

    .. versionchanged:: 1.1.0
        Blueprints have a ``cli`` group to register nested CLI commands.
        The ``cli_group`` parameter controls the name of the group under
        the ``flask`` command.

    .. versionadded:: 0.7
    """

    _got_registered_once = False

    def __init__(
        self,
        name: str,
        import_name: str,
        static_folder: t.Optional[t.Union[str, os.PathLike]] = None,
        static_url_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
        template_folder: t.Optional[t.Union[str, os.PathLike]] = None,
        url_prefix: t.Optional[str] = None,
        subdomain: t.Optional[str] = None,
        url_defaults: t.Optional[dict] = None,
        root_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
        cli_group: t.Optional[str] = _sentinel,  # type: ignore
    ):
        super().__init__(
            import_name=import_name,
            static_folder=static_folder,
            static_url_path=static_url_path,
            template_folder=template_folder,
            root_path=root_path,
        )

        if not name:
            raise ValueError("'name' may not be empty.")

        if "." in name:
            raise ValueError("'name' may not contain a dot '.' character.")

        self.name = name
        self.url_prefix = url_prefix
        self.subdomain = subdomain
        self.deferred_functions: t.List[DeferredSetupFunction] = []

        if url_defaults is None:
            url_defaults = {}

        self.url_values_defaults = url_defaults
        self.cli_group = cli_group
        self._blueprints: t.List[t.Tuple["Blueprint", dict]] = []

    def _check_setup_finished(self, f_name: str) -> None:
        if self._got_registered_once:
            raise AssertionError(
                f"The setup method '{f_name}' can no longer be called on the blueprint"
                f" '{self.name}'. It has already been registered at least once, any"
                " changes will not be applied consistently.\n"
                "Make sure all imports, decorators, functions, etc. needed to set up"
                " the blueprint are done before registering it."
            )

    @setupmethod
    def record(self, func: t.Callable) -> None:
        """Registers a function that is called when the blueprint is
        registered on the application.  This function is called with the
        state as argument as returned by the :meth:`make_setup_state`
        method.
        """
        self.deferred_functions.append(func)

    @setupmethod
    def record_once(self, func: t.Callable) -> None:
        """Works like :meth:`record` but wraps the function in another
        function that will ensure the function is only called once.  If the
        blueprint is registered a second time on the application, the
        function passed is not called.
        """

        def wrapper(state: BlueprintSetupState) -> None:
            if state.first_registration:
                func(state)

        self.record(update_wrapper(wrapper, func))

    def make_setup_state(
        self, app: "Flask", options: dict, first_registration: bool = False
    ) -> BlueprintSetupState:
        """Creates an instance of :meth:`~flask.blueprints.BlueprintSetupState`
        object that is later passed to the register callback functions.
        Subclasses can override this to return a subclass of the setup state.
        """
        return BlueprintSetupState(self, app, options, first_registration)

    @setupmethod
    def register_blueprint(self, blueprint: "Blueprint", **options: t.Any) -> None:
        """Register a :class:`~flask.Blueprint` on this blueprint. Keyword
        arguments passed to this method will override the defaults set
        on the blueprint.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.0.1
            The ``name`` option can be used to change the (pre-dotted)
            name the blueprint is registered with. This allows the same
            blueprint to be registered multiple times with unique names
            for ``url_for``.

        .. versionadded:: 2.0
        """
        if blueprint is self:
            raise ValueError("Cannot register a blueprint on itself")
        self._blueprints.append((blueprint, options))

    def register(self, app: "Flask", options: dict) -> None:
        """Called by :meth:`Flask.register_blueprint` to register all
        views and callbacks registered on the blueprint with the
        application. Creates a :class:`.BlueprintSetupState` and calls
        each :meth:`record` callback with it.

        :param app: The application this blueprint is being registered
            with.
        :param options: Keyword arguments forwarded from
            :meth:`~Flask.register_blueprint`.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.3
            Nested blueprints now correctly apply subdomains.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.1
            Registering the same blueprint with the same name multiple
            times is an error.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.0.1
            Nested blueprints are registered with their dotted name.
            This allows different blueprints with the same name to be
            nested at different locations.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.0.1
            The ``name`` option can be used to change the (pre-dotted)
            name the blueprint is registered with. This allows the same
            blueprint to be registered multiple times with unique names
            for ``url_for``.
        """
        name_prefix = options.get("name_prefix", "")
        self_name = options.get("name", self.name)
        name = f"{name_prefix}.{self_name}".lstrip(".")

        if name in app.blueprints:
            bp_desc = "this" if app.blueprints[name] is self else "a different"
            existing_at = f" '{name}'" if self_name != name else ""

            raise ValueError(
                f"The name '{self_name}' is already registered for"
                f" {bp_desc} blueprint{existing_at}. Use 'name=' to"
                f" provide a unique name."
            )

        first_bp_registration = not any(bp is self for bp in app.blueprints.values())
        first_name_registration = name not in app.blueprints

        app.blueprints[name] = self
        self._got_registered_once = True
        state = self.make_setup_state(app, options, first_bp_registration)

        if self.has_static_folder:
            state.add_url_rule(
                f"{self.static_url_path}/<path:filename>",
                view_func=self.send_static_file,
                endpoint="static",
            )

        # Merge blueprint data into parent.
        if first_bp_registration or first_name_registration:

            def extend(bp_dict, parent_dict):
                for key, values in bp_dict.items():
                    key = name if key is None else f"{name}.{key}"
                    parent_dict[key].extend(values)

            for key, value in self.error_handler_spec.items():

                value = defaultdict(

            extend(self.before_request_funcs, app.before_request_funcs)
            extend(self.after_request_funcs, app.after_request_funcs)

    def iter_blueprints(self) -> t.ValuesView["Blueprint"]:
        """Iterates over all blueprints by the order they were registered.

        .. versionadded:: 0.11
        """
        return self.blueprints.values()

    @setupmethod
    def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
        view_func: t.Optional[ft.RouteCallable] = None,
        provide_automatic_options: t.Optional[bool] = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        if endpoint is None:
            endpoint = _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func)  # type: ignore
        options["endpoint"] = endpoint
        methods = options.pop("methods", None)

        # if the methods are not given and the view_func object knows its
        # methods we can use that instead.  If neither exists, we go with
        # a tuple of only ``GET`` as default.
        if methods is None:
            methods = getattr(view_func, "methods", None) or ("GET",)
        if isinstance(methods, str):
            raise TypeError(
                "Allowed methods must be a list of strings, for"
                ' example: @app.route(..., methods=["POST"])'
            )
        methods = {item.upper() for item in methods}

        # Methods that should always be added
        required_methods = set(getattr(view_func, "required_methods", ()))

        # starting with Flask 0.8 the view_func object can disable and
        # force-enable the automatic options handling.
        if provide_automatic_options is None:
            provide_automatic_options = getattr(
                view_func, "provide_automatic_options", None
            )

        if provide_automatic_options is None:
            if "OPTIONS" not in methods:
                provide_automatic_options = True
                required_methods.add("OPTIONS")
            else:
                provide_automatic_options = False

        # Add the required methods now.
        methods |= required_methods

        rule = self.url_rule_class(rule, methods=methods, **options)
        rule.provide_automatic_options = provide_automatic_options  # type: ignore

        self.url_map.add(rule)
        if view_func is not None:
            old_func = self.view_functions.get(endpoint)
            if old_func is not None and old_func != view_func:
                raise AssertionError(
                    "View function mapping is overwriting an existing"
                    f" endpoint function: {endpoint}"
                )
            self.view_functions[endpoint] = view_func

    def template_filter(

  - create_url_adapter binds the url_map with server_name=self.config["SERVER_NAME"] and subdomain behavior depends on app.subdomain_matching. Flask uses the Werkzeug Map/Rule to perform actual matching with host/subdomain.

What hardcoded values / constants I noted in components
- app.default_config has 'SERVER_NAME': None by default. This config is what routes_command can use to form a full domain (SERVER_NAME + subdomain). No other hard constants relevant.
- Blueprints store a blueprint.subdomain attribute (default None) — blueprint-level default for subdomain.
- Werkzeug Rule/Map are external; Flask relies on them to hold host/subdomain data. (I cannot read Werkzeug source here, it's external to this repo.)

Sibling classes / patterns
- There are no sibling implementations of routes_command; it's the canonical CLI display implemented in Flask's cli module.

Call graph
- routes_command calls current_app.url_map.iter_rules() and current_app.config and constructs strings from rule attributes: rule.endpoint, rule.methods, rule.rule.

Key architectural detail relevant to the fix
- The authoritative storage for per-rule subdomain/host is the Rule instance that Flask constructs via self.url_rule_class(..., **options) (i.e., options might include 'subdomain' or 'host'). Blueprints pass 'subdomain' into options when registering. routes_command currently does not inspect any host/subdomain attributes on Rule instances; it only prints endpoint, methods, rule.rule.

Conclusion:
- To include domain/subdomain information we must enhance routes_command to attempt to extract a domain string for each rule. The best approach is to (in order) look for:
  - attributes on the Rule instance that indicate host or subdomain (common attribute names are 'host' and 'subdomain' depending on Werkzeug version),
  - fall back to combining current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"] with a subdomain attribute if present,
  - finally fall back to current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"] alone or empty string.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (complete)
- Modify src/flask/cli.py::routes_command to:
  1. Compute a domain string per rule by checking rule.host and rule.subdomain attributes (using getattr), and combining rule.subdomain with current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"] when appropriate.
  2. Add a "Domain" column to the printed table (so headers become Domain, Endpoint, Methods, Rule).
  3. Compute widths including the domain column and print rows with domain first.
- This is a purely display-level change to the CLI: it does not change how rules are stored or matched, only adds a column to the output.

Code change (replace the existing routes_command body with the following implementation):
(Note: I will present the exact new implementation to make the fix complete.)

Replace the current routes_command function in src/flask/cli.py with:

```
@click.command("routes", short_help="Show the routes for the app.")
@click.option(
    "--sort",
    "-s",
    type=click.Choice(("endpoint", "methods", "rule", "match")),
    default="endpoint",
    help=(
        'Method to sort routes by. "match" is the order that Flask will match '
        "routes when dispatching a request."
    ),
)
@click.option("--all-methods", is_flag=True, help="Show HEAD and OPTIONS methods.")
@with_appcontext
def routes_command(sort: str, all_methods: bool) -> None:
    """Show all registered routes with endpoints and methods."""

    rules = list(current_app.url_map.iter_rules())
    if not rules:
        click.echo("No routes were registered.")
        return

    ignored_methods = set(() if all_methods else ("HEAD", "OPTIONS"))

    if sort in ("endpoint", "rule"):
        rules = sorted(rules, key=attrgetter(sort))
    elif sort == "methods":
        rules = sorted(rules, key=lambda rule: sorted(rule.methods))  # type: ignore

    rule_methods = [
        ", ".join(sorted(rule.methods - ignored_methods))  # type: ignore
        for rule in rules
    ]

    # Build a domain string for each rule.
    server_name = current_app.config.get("SERVER_NAME") or ""
    def _domain_for_rule(rule):
        # Try common attributes on Rule (Werkzeug compatibility).
        host = getattr(rule, "host", None)
        subdomain = getattr(rule, "subdomain", None)

        if subdomain:
            # If SERVER_NAME present, prepend it (keep any port).
            if server_name:
                return f"{subdomain}.{server_name}"
            return subdomain
        if host:
            return host
        if server_name:
            return server_name
        return ""

    domains = [_domain_for_rule(rule) for rule in rules]

    headers = ("Domain", "Endpoint", "Methods", "Rule")
    widths = (
        max(len(d) for d in domains),
        max(len(rule.endpoint) for rule in rules),
        max(len(methods) for methods in rule_methods),
        max(len(rule.rule) for rule in rules),
    )
    widths = [max(len(h), w) for h, w in zip(headers, widths)]
    row = "{{0:<{0}}}  {{1:<{1}}}  {{2:<{2}}}  {{3:<{3}}}".format(*widths)

    click.echo(row.format(*headers).strip())
    click.echo(row.format(*("-" * width for width in widths)))

    for rule, methods, domain in zip(rules, rule_methods, domains):
        click.echo(row.format(domain, rule.endpoint, methods, rule.rule).rstrip())
```

(If you prefer a minimal change view: the key changes are: compute domains, add the domain column to headers and widths, update row format and the final printed line.)

Now I will work through the required questions (4a–4e), referencing the code I examined.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The current CLI assumed the domain/subdomain for a route was not displayed (or not easily derivable), and the fix changes that assumption by reading host/subdomain attributes from each Rule (or combining the registered subdomain with SERVER_NAME) and displaying a "Domain" column in the routes table.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The main "assumption" was encoded implicitly by having a 3-column output (headers = ("Endpoint", "Methods", "Rule")) and widths computed only for those three items (cli.py: headers = ("Endpoint", "Methods", "Rule"); widths tuple built from rule.endpoint, rule_methods, rule.rule).
- Regarding hardcoded constants I noted in component classes that might encode assumptions that affect the fix:
  - app.default_config["SERVER_NAME"] = None (in app.py). If SERVER_NAME is None, we cannot show a full host like admin.test.local; in that case we show either the rule.host/rule.subdomain or empty.
  - Blueprints: blueprint.subdomain default is None (in blueprints.py).
  - No other hardcoded numbers/values in Flask/Blueprints affect this CLI change.
- Are these values still correct if we display domain?
  - SERVER_NAME default None: still correct; our code handles None by using rule.host or rule.subdomain or leaving the domain empty. No hardcoded value needs to change.
  - No other hardcoded values in the component classes encode an assumption that we are changing, so they do not need to become dynamic.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The "reverse operation" is the code paths that create and store route subdomain/host info: Flask.add_url_rule and BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule. I re-read them to confirm we are only reading data they already set.
- Tracing the relevant code:
  - BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule sets options.setdefault("subdomain", self.subdomain) then calls self.app.add_url_rule(..., **options) (blueprints.py lines ~96–114).
    * Variables: when a blueprint is registered with subdomain='admin', state.subdomain == 'admin', and options.setdefault('subdomain', 'admin') will ensure Flask.add_url_rule receives subdomain='admin'.
  - Flask.add_url_rule receives **options which may include 'subdomain' or 'host'. It then calls:
    rule = self.url_rule_class(rule, methods=methods, **options)
    self.url_map.add(rule)
    (app.py lines ~1025–1068)
  - The Rule instance produced by url_rule_class (werkzeug.Rule) is the authoritative store for host/subdomain matching. routes_command now attempts to read rule.host or rule.subdomain attributes. This is a read-only operation; we are not changing how rules are created.
- Concrete substitution examples:
  - Example: blueprint registered with subdomain='admin' and app.config["SERVER_NAME"] == 'test.local'.
    * When registering: BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule passes options['subdomain'] = 'admin'
    * Flask.add_url_rule calls url_rule_class(..., subdomain='admin') -> Rule instance containing subdomain info (host or subdomain attribute).
    * In routes_command: rule = that Rule instance.
        - getattr(rule, "subdomain", None) -> probably 'admin' (if Werkzeug stores it as 'subdomain')
        - server_name = 'test.local'
        - domain = f"admin.test.local"
      That is correct: the displayed domain will be admin.test.local.
  - If Werkzeug instead stores the value as rule.host (for example when host matching is used), getattr(rule, "host", None) will return a host pattern (maybe 'admin.test.local' or '<subdomain>.test.local'), and we display that as domain. So either path should give a reasonable human-readable domain.
- Conclusion: Because we only read attributes exposed by the Rule object (falling back to SERVER_NAME when needed), I have verified the complementary operations (the `add_url_rule` call that stores subdomain info) are still compatible. No write-side changes were made that would require updates to the read-side elsewhere.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods/functions we changed: only routes_command (cli.py). This function's external behavior is extended (it prints an additional Domain column). Its existing functionality still works:
  - Sorting rules by endpoint/methods/rule still done before building rule_methods and domain strings — unchanged.
  - Methods computation and ignored_methods logic is unchanged.
  - No other functions in the repo are modified.
- No other methods in the primary file are impacted (run_command, shell_command, etc remain unchanged).

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new module-level methods are strictly required. I wrote an internal helper function _domain_for_rule inside routes_command (local function) for clarity in the suggested implementation. This is a small helper, not necessary to be added elsewhere.
- Consideration about parent class methods: routes_command lives in cli.py and is not a class method that requires matching in a parent. No inherited methods touched.
- Check for methods that operate on related state:
  - Flask.add_url_rule remains as-is: it already stores subdomain/host in the Rule. We are reading those rule attributes.
  - Flask.create_url_adapter and url_for are unaffected.
- Therefore no new methods are required in Flask or Blueprint classes.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis + every location that needs to change)

Root cause
- routes_command (src/flask/cli.py) does not inspect route host/subdomain information because it only prints Endpoint, Methods, Rule (path). Blueprint subdomain information is passed into Flask.add_url_rule options (and stored on the Rule by werkzeug), but the CLI printing code never looked there and therefore could not display the route's domain/subdomain.

Complete fix (locations and changes)
1) Primary change (required)
- File: src/flask/cli.py
- Change: Update the routes_command function to compute a "Domain" string for each rule and add a "Domain" column to the printed table.
- Rationale: This is the CLI command that lists routes. Showing domain information here is the most direct fix.

Implementation details (what to change in code)
- Compute domain per rule:
  - Check rule.host attribute first (getattr(rule, "host", None))
  - Then check rule.subdomain attribute (getattr(rule, "subdomain", None))
  - If subdomain exists and SERVER_NAME is configured, join subdomain and SERVER_NAME (e.g. f"{subdomain}.{SERVER_NAME}")
  - Fallback to SERVER_NAME if present, otherwise empty string
- Add header "Domain" and include domain column in width calculations and formatting.
- Print the table with columns [Domain, Endpoint, Methods, Rule].

Why no other locations need to change
- Blueprint registration (blueprints.py) and Flask.add_url_rule (app.py) already pass subdomain/host data into Rule creation; I validated these call sites and they are already correct. The issue is purely presentation in the CLI. We do not need to change storage or matching logic.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None of the hardcoded constants in Flask or Blueprint classes need to be changed. The only value that matters is app.config["SERVER_NAME"] and we already read it dynamically in the updated CLI. The default of SERVER_NAME = None remains appropriate.

Potential edge cases and notes
- Different Werkzeug versions may expose host/subdomain on Rule instances under different attribute names. The fix attempts to be robust by checking both rule.host and rule.subdomain (via getattr). In the unlikely case neither attribute exists (or is None) and SERVER_NAME is None, the Domain column will be empty for that route. This is the safest behavior.
- When SERVER_NAME includes a port (e.g. "dev.local:5000"), combining subdomain with SERVER_NAME will include the port (e.g. "admin.dev.local:5000"). This seems reasonable; if desired you could strip the port when displaying, but that is a presentation choice and not done here.
- Nested blueprints and blueprint-level defaults: Flask allows registering the same blueprint with different registration options (name, subdomain). The authoritative per-registration subdomain is stored in the Rule instance (via the Rule created in add_url_rule). We read from the Rule, not from blueprint.subdomain, because the blueprint object alone doesn't contain registration-specific overrides.

Testing and verification
- Manual verification:
  - If you create an app with app.config["SERVER_NAME"] = "test.local", register two blueprints with subdomain="admin" and subdomain="test", and run flask routes, the new output should include a Domain column showing admin.test.local and test.test.local for the corresponding endpoints.
- Automated tests:
  - Add unit tests that call the routes CLI and assert the Domain column contains expected values for rules registered with subdomain via blueprints and static routes with host set.
  - Tests should cover:
    * Blueprint subdomain + SERVER_NAME set
    * Rule created with host argument (static host) and SERVER_NAME None
    * No SERVER_NAME and no host/subdomain -> Domain is empty

Summary (concise)
- Problem: cli.routes_command didn't show per-route domain/subdomain information.
- Fix: Update src/flask/cli.py::routes_command to compute and display a domain column by inspecting each rule's host/subdomain attributes and falling back to SERVER_NAME.
- Scope: Only the CLI display function changes; Flask and Blueprint registration/storage code already provide the necessary data. The fix is backwards-compatible and safe across Werkzeug versions by checking both likely attribute names.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a patch/diff in unified format for the repository with the exact lines to change.
- Add unit tests (in tests/) to assert the new "Domain" column appears and contains expected values for example setups.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

The wrong assumption: the CLI assumed domain/host data is not needed or available for display.
- Root cause & location: routes_command in src/flask/cli.py builds the routes table but only reads rule.endpoint, rule.methods and rule.rule; it never inspects rule.host or rule.subdomain or app.config["SERVER_NAME"]. (Findings from Checkpoints 1–2.)
- Fix strategy: change src/flask/cli.py::routes_command to compute a "Domain" string per Rule and add a "Domain" column to the printed table. Domain is computed by checking Rule attributes and falling back to SERVER_NAME: domain = rule.host OR (rule.subdomain + "." + SERVER_NAME) OR SERVER_NAME. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4.)
- Scope: single CLI display change. No changes required to registration/storage code because blueprints and Flask already pass subdomain/host into Rule creation (evidence in src/flask/blueprints.py and src/flask/app.py). (Checked in Checkpoint 3.)
- Hardcoded constants: none need to change. The code must read current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"] at runtime; default SERVER_NAME = None remains valid.
- Gaps in investigation: the agent did not inspect the external werkzeug.routing.Rule implementation to confirm exact attribute names and semantics (e.g., whether host or subdomain is present). The fix uses getattr(rule, "host", None) and getattr(rule, "subdomain", None) to be robust, but this compatibility requires verification/test coverage. (Gap flagged in Checkpoint 4.)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: CLI omission — routes_command doesn't surface domain data (Checkpoints 1–2)
What: src/flask/cli.py::routes_command builds the routes table but only uses rule.endpoint, rule.methods and rule.rule; it never inspects rule.host or rule.subdomain or current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"]. Why it matters: CLI output lacks domain/subdomain column so users cannot map endpoints to admin.test.local vs test.test.local.

### Finding 2: Blueprints pass subdomain into add_url_rule (Checkpoint 3)
What: src/flask/blueprints.py::BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule calls options.setdefault("subdomain", self.subdomain) before calling app.add_url_rule(..., **options). Why it matters: blueprint registration already supplies per-registration subdomain into Flask.add_url_rule; the data necessary to display domains is present at rule-creation time.

### Finding 3: Flask forwards subdomain/host into Rule creation (Checkpoint 3)
What: src/flask/app.py::Flask.add_url_rule constructs the Rule via self.url_rule_class(rule, methods=..., **options) and self.url_map.add(rule). Why it matters: the Rule instance (werkzeug.routing.Rule) is the authoritative place where host/subdomain is stored — CLI should read from the Rule, not from Blueprint objects.

### Finding 4: Proposed domain-computation and table change (Checkpoint 4)
What: Modify src/flask/cli.py::routes_command to:
- compute per-rule domain using (in order) getattr(rule, "host", None), getattr(rule, "subdomain", None) combined with current_app.config["SERVER_NAME"], or fallback to SERVER_NAME;
- add "Domain" column to headers and width calculations; print rows as Domain, Endpoint, Methods, Rule.
Why it matters: this addresses the user request directly and is localized to the CLI.

### Finding 5: Investigation gap — werkzeug.Rule attributes not confirmed (Checkpoint 4)
What: The repo investigation did not open external werkzeug.routing.Rule source to confirm attribute names and formats (host vs subdomain, presence of variables, port handling). Why it matters: display code must be robust across Werkzeug versions; using getattr for both 'host' and 'subdomain' is a pragmatic fix but needs explicit verification and unit tests.

### Finding 6: Reverse-path traced but attribute-level confirmation missing (Checkpoint 4c)
What: The agent tracked blueprint -> BlueprintSetupState -> Flask.add_url_rule flow with a concrete example (blueprint subdomain='admin' + SERVER_NAME='test.local' => domain admin.test.local), but did not inspect the actual Rule instance attributes created by Werkzeug in this runtime. Why it matters: without confirming the exact attribute(s) set on Rule, the CLI read may miss host/subdomain in some environments.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/flask/cli.py:routes_command
- Wrong assumption: routes_command assumes the only useful per-rule printable fields are endpoint, methods and path (rule.rule); it omits host/subdomain data.
- Evidence: routes_command implementation reads rule.endpoint, formats rule.methods and rule.rule; headers variable is ("Endpoint","Methods","Rule") and widths are computed only from those three values (file read in Checkpoint 2).
- Related locations: src/flask/blueprints.py::BlueprintSetupState.add_url_rule, src/flask/app.py::Flask.add_url_rule (these produce/store the subdomain data that routes_command should read).

Exact change to implement (preserve variable names and formula):
- Compute server_name = current_app.config.get("SERVER_NAME") or ""
- Per-rule domain algorithm (exact formula/pseudocode to include verbatim):

    host = getattr(rule, "host", None)
    subdomain = getattr(rule, "subdomain", None)

Result and analysis:
- I inspected src/flask/cli.py (lines shown below). The routes_command implementation is at src/flask/cli.py:1001 onwards.
  - It collects rules: rules = list(current_app.url_map.iter_rules()) (line 1004).
  - It computes methods for display: rule_methods = [", ".join(sorted(rule.methods - ignored_methods)) for rule in rules] (lines 1016–1019).

bug_report.Title: flask routes CLI omits per-route domain/subdomain information
bug_report.Description: The `flask routes` CLI prints endpoint, methods and path but does not show which domain or subdomain each route is bound to (for example admin.test.local vs test.test.local). When an application sets SERVER_NAME and registers blueprints with a subdomain option, the CLI output does not expose that per-route host/subdomain information, preventing users from mapping routes to configured subdomains from the CLI output alone.
StepsToReproduce: Set the app SERVER_NAME to 'test.local' (e.g. app.config['SERVER_NAME'] = 'test.local' or via environment).

Register blueprints with subdomains, e.g. app.register_blueprint(admin_blueprint, url_prefix='', subdomain='admin') and app.register_blueprint(test_subdomain_blueprint, url_prefix='', subdomain='test').
Run the Flask CLI command: flask routes
Observe that the output lists Endpoint, Methods and Rule but does not show domain/subdomain information for each route.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The `flask routes` output includes a Domain column (or otherwise shows per-route host/subdomain) that identifies the domain or subdomain for each route (for example 'admin.test.local', 'test.test.local', or the app SERVER_NAME for host-level rules), so users can see which routes are assigned to which subdomain from the CLI output.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior:
The `flask routes` output only shows Endpoint, Methods and Rule (path). Example:

Endpoint                                                 Methods    Rule
-------------------------------------------------------  ---------  ------------------------------------------------
admin_blueprint.home                                      GET        /home
test_subdomain_blueprint.home                             GET        /home
static                                                    GET        /static/<path:filename>
...

No domain/subdomain column is printed, so it is not possible from this output to tell which endpoints are bound to admin.test.local vs test.test.local.
